title: Random Forests and Gradient Boosting
summary: Ensemble methods that combine weak learners into a strong one. Dominant on tabular data.
key_concepts: Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregation): train on random subsets, average predictions
Random Forest: bagging + random feature subsets at each split
Boosting: sequential ensemble, each tree corrects previous errors
Gradient Boosting: fit new tree to negative gradient of loss
XGBoost: regularized gradient boosting with second-order optimization
ml_connection: XGBoost/LightGBM win most Kaggle tabular competitions. Random Forest is robust baseline. Both are standard industry tools.
exercises: Implement Random Forest from scratch using your Decision Tree.
Explain why bagging reduces variance but not bias.
What hyperparameters matter most in XGBoost? (n_estimators, max_depth, learning_rate, subsample)
Implement feature importance from a trained Random Forest.
code_example:
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
import xgboost as xgb
import numpy as np

# Random Forest
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, max_features='sqrt', random_state=42)

# XGBoost
xgb_model = xgb.XGBClassifier(
    n_estimators=200, max_depth=6, learning_rate=0.1,
    subsample=0.8, colsample_bytree=0.8, random_state=42)

# Feature importance
rf.fit(X_train, y_train)
importances = rf.feature_importances_
thinking_questions: Why does Random Forest use sqrt(n_features) at each split?
When would you choose Random Forest over XGBoost?
